name: La Sierra de la Cabrera y el antiguo tren Madrid-Burgos — Sierra de Guadarrama
region_intro: Mountain biking around La Cabrera, located in the Community of Madrid, Spain, features an impressive granite massif and diverse trails. The region offers varied terrain, from winding roads to steep climbs, with sections of flowy trails and rocky segments. Routes connect traditional villages, providing a blend of natural and cultural features for mountain bikers. Best mountain bike trails around La Cabrera The most popular mountain bike trail is La Sierra de la Cabrera y el antiguo tren Madrid-Burgos — Sierra de Guadarrama, a 15.5 miles (24.9 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 28 minutes to complete. This route follows an old train line through the Sierra de Guadarrama. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Railway Workers' Housing Ruins – View of Puerto de Canencia loop from La Cabrera, a difficult 34.0 miles (54.7 km) path. This challenging route features significant elevation gain and unpaved segments. Local mountain bikers also love the El Berrueco – Visigothic Hermitage of Valcárnamo loop from Cabanillas de la Sierra, a 23.1 miles (37.2 km) trail leading through traditional villages and natural landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 22 minutes. Mountain biking around La Cabrera is defined by its granite massif, diverse trails, and traditional villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to challenging routes. The routes in La Cabrera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 220 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore La Cabrera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in La Cabrera?
A: This guide features over 20 mountain bike trails around La Cabrera, catering to various skill levels. The wider Sierra de La Cabrera region is known for hundreds of trails, offering extensive options for exploration.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in La Cabrera?
A: La Cabrera's mountain bike trails are characterized by an impressive granite massif, offering diverse terrain. You'll encounter everything from winding roads and steep climbs to 'flowy' sections with granite slabs and rocky segments. Some routes may even require pushing your bike due to their challenging nature.

Q: Are there any easy mountain bike trails suitable for beginners in La Cabrera?
A: Yes, there are options for beginners. While many trails offer significant challenges, you can find easier rides on mostly paved surfaces. This guide includes 2 easy routes, alongside 14 moderate and 4 difficult ones, ensuring there's something for every skill level.

Q: What are some of the more challenging mountain bike routes in La Cabrera?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10418564" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Railway Workers' Housing Ruins – View of Puerto de Canencia loop from La Cabrera&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain and unpaved segments. This particular route is rated as difficult and covers over 54 km.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about the trails in La Cabrera?
A: The komoot community highly rates the mountain biking in La Cabrera, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 10 reviews. Riders often praise the diverse trails, the stunning granite massif, and the blend of natural beauty with traditional villages.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in La Cabrera?
A: Yes, many of the trails in La Cabrera are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31525192" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Berrueco – Visigothic Hermitage of Valcárnamo loop from Cabanillas de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through traditional villages and natural landscapes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the mountain bike trails?
A: The trails often pass through stunning natural landscapes. You might encounter highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho de la Cabeza Summit&lt;/a&gt;, the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2791690" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Villar Reservoir and Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, or the expansive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6217974" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Atazar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The region's granite massif provides unique geological formations and panoramic views.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions to explore near the mountain bike routes?
A: Absolutely. The area is rich in rural and cultural heritage. Many routes connect traditional villages like La Cabrera, El Berrueco, Sieteiglesias, and Las Navas de Buitrago. You can also visit places like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4606981" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Patones de Arriba&lt;/a&gt;, known for its well-preserved architecture, or the Visigothic Hermitage of Valcárnamo.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in La Cabrera?
A: La Cabrera offers good mountain biking conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant temperatures for riding. While winter can bring challenging conditions, the region's diverse trails mean there are often options available, though some higher elevation routes may be affected by weather.

Q: Is it possible to reach the mountain biking trails in La Cabrera using public transport?
A: La Cabrera is generally accessible, including by public transport from Madrid. Check local bus schedules for routes connecting to the villages in the Sierra de La Cabrera, which serve as starting points for many trails.

Q: Are there specific parking areas for mountain bikers in La Cabrera?
A: While specific dedicated mountain bike parking areas aren't always signposted, you can typically find parking in or near the traditional villages that serve as trailheads, such as La Cabrera, El Berrueco, or Cabanillas de la Sierra. Always ensure you park responsibly and respect local regulations.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails in La Cabrera?
A: Yes, La Cabrera offers routes suitable for families. While some trails are challenging, there are easier rides on mostly paved surfaces that can be enjoyed by families. Look for routes with lower difficulty ratings and less elevation gain for a more relaxed experience.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails in La Cabrera?
A: Many trails in natural areas of Spain, including La Cabrera, are generally dog-friendly, especially if your dog is well-behaved and kept on a leash. However, it's always advisable to check specific local regulations or signage for individual trails or protected areas to ensure dogs are permitted.
tips: Near the town of La Cabrera we find this Visigoth Necropolis. Dated in the s. VII consists of an anthropomorphic grave and nine simple grave graves, two of which are very similar to each other, stipulating that they belong to a married couple. A curious place to stop.

Along our way through the Cañada Real Segoviana near the Sierra de la Cabrera, we began to see it with the Cancho Gordo as a presentation. This track is very beautiful in spring or after a few days of rain, when everything is green.

Mountain pass located north of Valdemanco in the Cañada Real Segoviana that runs between the Sierra de la Cabrera and the Sierra de Canencia. Large place to enjoy the landscape and rest briefly before beginning the descent.

This abandoned 2,300 meter long railway tunnel is located on the old Madrid-Burgos direct line. This line, inaugurated in 1967, has a tedious construction history, with the first projects emerging at the end of the 20th century. XIX, with several failed plans at the beginning of the s. XX and with its construction beginning in 1928. Currently, this section of track is not used, the trains circulate on another route, which makes passage through this dark tunnel safe, as long as flashlights are used inside it.

After the civil war, a major project was undertaken to link Madrid with Burgos with the railway. These homes complement the "Lozoyuela prison" since numerous prisoners worked there. These homes were those that belonged to the managers, guards and relatives of the prisoners.
